content.text: Partner and Reseller Vendor Assessment

Enterprises that deploy Claude through partners, resellers, or system integrators face a more complex vendor landscape than those with a direct Anthropic contract. A partner may add capabilities on top of the Claude API (a no-code AI builder, a customer-facing chatbot platform, or a managed inference service), or may simply resell Anthropic's enterprise tier. In either case, your security and procurement teams must assess the partner as an independent vendor risk, separate from the Anthropic due diligence already completed.

attrs.alt: Partner and Reseller Vendor Assessment Flow

content.text: Partner Types and Their Risk Profiles
Not all Claude partners carry the same risk. The first step in assessment is classifying the partner's relationship to your data.
Partner Type
Data Exposure
Risk Tier
Examples
Sub-processor / platform
Sees prompt and response content; may persist logs, fine-tuning data, or user sessions
Tier 1 — High
AI platform vendors (e.g. a chatbot SaaS built on Claude) where your users' data passes through the vendor's infrastructure
System integrator (SI)
Builds your Claude integration; has access to your system prompts, API keys, and potentially production data during development
Tier 2 — Medium
Consulting firms implementing Claude pipelines; offshore development teams building RAG integrations
Tooling vendor
Provides SDKs, monitoring, or orchestration tooling that wraps the Claude API; may capture traces or telemetry
Tier 2 — Medium
LLM observability platforms (e.g. LangSmith, Helicone); prompt management tools
Reseller
No access to data; purely commercial relationship to purchase Anthropic contracts at negotiated rates
Tier 3 — Low
Value-added resellers (VARs) on cloud marketplaces; Anthropic channel partners
Referral partner
No data access; commercial introduction only
Tier 3 — Low
Advisory firms that recommend Anthropic as part of an AI strategy engagement

The key question is: does this partner's infrastructure ever process, transit, or store data that originated from your systems or your users? If yes, they are at minimum Tier 2 and likely Tier 1. A reseller that only handles the invoice is Tier 3 regardless of how they describe themselves.
Tier 1 Assessment: Sub-Processors and Platform Vendors

Tier 1 partners require the most thorough assessment because they sit in the data path between your systems and the Claude API. Many SaaS products built on Claude act as sub-processors of the end enterprise customer. If your organization uses one of these platforms, the platform vendor must be assessed as if it were a primary cloud vendor.
Required Artifacts for Tier 1
SOC 2 Type II report:
the platform vendor's own SOC 2, not Anthropic's. Request the full report under NDA; focus on the same criteria as the Anthropic assessment: exceptions, CUECs, observation period.
Data Processing Agreement (DPA):

you are the data controller; the partner is the sub-processor. The DPA must cover the categories of data processed, retention policy, training data use restrictions, and breach notification obligations to you.
Sub-processor list for the partner:
the partner will themselves use sub-processors (typically Anthropic, AWS, and others). You need to know who those are and confirm they are acceptable to your security team.
Penetration test summary:
annual third-party pen test. Ask about any open P0/P1 findings.
Vendor security questionnaire:
SIG Lite or equivalent. Most Tier 1 vendors maintain pre-completed versions.
Incident response obligations:
confirm the notification SLA in the DPA (how quickly they notify you of a breach affecting your data).
Training Data Opt-Out

A critical control for Tier 1 partners is whether the partner uses your data to train or fine-tune their own models, or whether they share it with Anthropic for model training. This is distinct from Anthropic's own training data policy. If the platform vendor processes user conversations to improve their product, your DPA must explicitly prohibit this for data from your account.

## Tier 1 Partner DPA Checklist

Data Processing Terms:
- [ ] DPA executed before sending any production data to the partner
- [ ] DPA specifies: categories of personal data, purpose limitation, retention period
- [ ] Training data opt-out clause: partner may not use our data for model training
- [ ] Sub-processor list included as annex; change notification mechanism confirmed

Data Security:
- [ ] Encryption in transit (TLS 1.2+) and at rest confirmed
- [ ] API key / credential management: partner must not store our Anthropic API keys
- [ ] Logical tenant isolation confirmed (our data not commingled with other customers)
- [ ] Audit log access: can we retrieve logs of data access for forensics?

Breach and Incident:
- [ ] Breach notification to us within 48h of partner becoming aware (or per applicable law)
- [ ] Incident response contact and escalation path documented

Termination and Portability:
- [ ] Data deletion within 30 days of contract termination confirmed
- [ ] Export format for any stored data (conversations, fine-tuning datasets) specified
Tier 2 Assessment: System Integrators and Tooling Vendors

System integrators and tooling vendors have indirect data exposure — they build or instrument your Claude integration rather than operating in the production data path. The risk is concentrated in the development and deployment phase rather than ongoing operations.
System Integrator Controls

When an SI builds your Claude integration, they will have access to your system prompts, API keys, and potentially test data containing real user data. The assessment focuses on how the SI manages secrets and access during the engagement:
Credential handling:

confirm the SI uses your secrets manager (not local .env files or their own key vault) for API keys. Any Anthropic API keys used during development should be scoped to non-production and rotated at engagement close.
Background checks:
for engagements where the SI will access production data, confirm their background-checking policy for the specific personnel assigned.
Data handling for test data:
if test data includes real user PII, confirm the SI has a data handling agreement covering how that data is stored, used, and destroyed at engagement end.
Code ownership and IP:
confirm that all code produced during the engagement (including prompt templates) is owned by your organization, not licensed back from the SI.
Security review of delivered code:
any Claude integration code delivered by an SI should go through your standard SAST/DAST pipeline before production deployment. Do not assume the SI's own QA is sufficient.
Tooling Vendor Controls (LLM Observability / Prompt Management)

LLM observability and prompt management tools capture traces of Claude API calls — including prompts and responses — for debugging, evaluation, and cost tracking. If these tools are deployed in production, they become Tier 1 sub-processors and should be assessed accordingly. If deployed only in development environments, Tier 2 assessment is sufficient.

# Before wiring up an LLM observability platform, confirm data routing:
# - Does the tool send full prompt+response traces to its own backend?
# - Or does it operate as a local proxy/SDK that stores traces only in your infra?

# Example: LangSmith (LangChain's tracing tool)
# By default, traces are sent to api.smith.langchain.com
# For enterprises: confirm you have a private deployment or explicit data handling agreement

from langchain_anthropic import ChatAnthropic
from langchain_core.tracers import LangChainTracer

# Option A: Disable tracing entirely for regulated data
llm = ChatAnthropic(model="claude-opus-4-8")
# (no tracer attached)

# Option B: Use only if data handling agreement in place
# tracer = LangChainTracer(project_name="my-project")
# llm_with_trace = llm.with_config(callbacks=[tracer])

# Regardless: never include PII/PHI in trace metadata fields
# (even when traces are stored locally)
Tier 3 Assessment: Resellers and Referral Partners

Resellers and referral partners present minimal security risk because they do not touch your data. The due diligence focus shifts from security controls to commercial and legal risk: are you confident the partner has a legitimate reseller agreement with Anthropic, and will the contract terms they pass through to you be enforceable?
Assessment Area
Key Question
How to Verify
Legitimate reseller status
Does the partner have a current, valid reseller agreement with Anthropic?
Ask the partner for written confirmation; contact your Anthropic account executive to validate the reseller relationship
Contract pass-through
Are the Anthropic usage policies and acceptable use terms passed through to you intact?
Review the reseller's order form — it should reference Anthropic's master terms, not replace them
Pricing integrity
Is the pricing consistent with what you could obtain directly from Anthropic?
Request a price comparison from your Anthropic account executive; resellers should not charge a premium without clear value-add
Renewal and termination
If the reseller's agreement with Anthropic expires, what happens to your contract?
Confirm the order form includes a direct continuation right with Anthropic if the reseller relationship ends
Ongoing Partner Risk Management
Partner assessments are not one-time events. The vendor risk lifecycle requires periodic re-assessment, especially after security incidents or significant product changes.
Re-Assessment Triggers
Annual contract renewal: all Tier 1 and Tier 2 partners re-assessed at renewal, minimum.
Significant scope change: if a Tier 2 partner moves into the production data path (e.g., their tooling is deployed in production), re-classify to Tier 1 and perform full assessment.
Security incident at the partner: any reported breach or P0 security event requires an ad hoc re-assessment before determining whether to continue the relationship.
Material change in partner's product: if the partner significantly changes how they handle data (new feature, new sub-processor, new data region), request an updated DPA and sub-processor list.
Acquisition of the partner: if the partner is acquired by a third party, re-assess the acquiring entity's security controls.
Maintaining the Partner Register

Your organization should maintain a vendor register that includes all Claude-adjacent partners, their tier, assessment date, next review date, and contract status. This register supports audit evidence for SOC 2, ISO 27001, and regulatory reviews.

## Sample Partner Register Entry

| Field              | Value                                    |
|--------------------|------------------------------------------|
| Partner name       | AcmeChatOps Inc.                         |
| Partner type       | Platform vendor (chatbot SaaS on Claude) |
| Risk tier          | Tier 1 — High                            |
| Data exposure      | Processes customer chat transcripts      |
| Last assessed      | 2025-11-01                               |
| Next review        | 2026-11-01                               |
| SOC 2 on file      | Yes — expires 2026-03-15                 |
| DPA executed       | Yes — MSA Exhibit B, signed 2025-11-01   |
| Training opt-out   | Confirmed in DPA § 4.3                   |
| Incident contact   | security@acmechatops.com                 |
| Open findings      | None                                     |
| Contract status    | Active — renewal due 2026-01-01          |
Contractual Protections in Partner Agreements

Security assessments establish whether a partner meets your baseline controls, but the contract is what makes those controls enforceable. Many organizations complete thorough questionnaires and then execute a standard MSA that does not require the partner to maintain those controls. The security exhibit or data security addendum closes this gap by making the assessed controls contractual obligations.
Security Exhibit Contents
For Tier 1 partners, the security exhibit should cover:
Minimum security standards:

the partner must maintain at minimum the controls attested to at the time of assessment (e.g., SOC 2 Type II, encryption at rest and in transit, access controls). Material degradation of those controls must be disclosed.
Right to audit:
your organization has the right to request a current SOC 2 report or equivalent attestation annually, and to conduct a security questionnaire review at any time following a reportable security event.
Incident notification:

the partner must notify you within 48 hours of becoming aware of any security event that may affect your data. 'May affect' is deliberately broad — it is the partner's obligation to err on the side of disclosure.
Personnel security:

partners whose staff have access to production systems or non-anonymized data must maintain background check programs for those staff. Subcontracting to fourth parties who do not meet these standards is prohibited without prior written consent.
Termination and data deletion:
upon contract termination, the partner must delete all data received from your organization within 30 days and provide written confirmation. Backup media must be overwritten within 90 days.

## Security Exhibit Checklist — Tier 1 Partner Contract

Section 1: Security Standards
- [ ] Partner maintains SOC 2 Type II or equivalent certification
- [ ] Encryption in transit (TLS 1.2+) and at rest (AES-256 or equivalent)
- [ ] Multi-factor authentication required for privileged access to systems processing our data
- [ ] Vulnerability scanning at least monthly; critical findings remediated within 15 days

Section 2: Data Handling
- [ ] No training or model fine-tuning on our data without prior written consent
- [ ] Data segregation: our data not commingled with other customers in storage or processing
- [ ] Subprocessor changes require 30-day advance notice; we have right to object

Section 3: Audit and Reporting
- [ ] Annual SOC 2 or equivalent attestation provided to us within 60 days of issuance
- [ ] Security questionnaire responses updated within 10 business days of material change
- [ ] Right to conduct security assessment (questionnaire or third-party audit) annually

Section 4: Incident Management
- [ ] Security incident notification within 48 hours of discovery
- [ ] Incident response plan available upon request
- [ ] Post-incident report provided for P0 events within 30 days

Section 5: Termination
- [ ] Data deletion within 30 days of termination; confirmation provided in writing
- [ ] No post-termination processing of our data for any purpose
- [ ] Cooperation with transition to successor vendor
Common Assessment Failure Modes

Partner assessments frequently fail not because the partner is insecure, but because the assessment process itself breaks down. Understanding these failure modes helps procurement and security teams design a process that actually produces reliable risk information.
Failure Mode
What Happens
Prevention
Assessment theater
The partner completes a questionnaire but answers are not verified against evidence. High scores do not reflect actual controls.
Request the SOC 2 report and compare it to questionnaire answers. Discrepancies require explanation.
One-size questionnaire
A generic IT vendor questionnaire is applied to an AI sub-processor. Model-specific risks (prompt injection, training data use) are not covered.
Augment standard questionnaires with AI-specific sections covering prompt data handling, training opt-out, and model security.
No contract follow-through
Assessment finds controls in place; the contract contains no security exhibit. Assessed controls are not enforceable.
Legal review must confirm security exhibit is included before contract signature.
Point-in-time complacency
Assessment is completed at onboarding; no re-assessment is scheduled. Partner's security posture degrades over time undetected.
Diarize annual re-assessment at contract signature; make it a contract renewal prerequisite.
Tier misclassification
A Tier 2 tooling vendor is deployed in production, becoming a de facto Tier 1 sub-processor. Assessment gaps are not caught.
Require notification to security team before any vendor's deployment scope changes; re-classify and re-assess on change.

The most dangerous partner risk is not the one you assessed and found acceptable — it is the one you classified as Tier 3 that quietly became Tier 1 when your development team started sending production traffic through their debugging tool.
AI-Specific Questionnaire Additions

Standard vendor security questionnaires (SIG, CAIQ, VSAQ) were designed before LLM-based services were common. They do not adequately cover the risks specific to AI platform vendors. The following questions should be added to any questionnaire sent to a Tier 1 or Tier 2 Claude-adjacent vendor:

## AI-Specific Vendor Questionnaire Additions

Prompt and Response Data:
1. Do you log prompt and/or response content for any purpose (debugging, abuse detection,
   model evaluation, product analytics)? If yes, what is the retention period and who
   has access?
2. Do you use prompt/response data to train or fine-tune models (your own or third-party)?
   What is the opt-out mechanism?
3. Can customers request deletion of specific prompt/response logs under GDPR Art. 17?
   What is the documented process?

Model Security:
4. How do you test for and mitigate prompt injection vulnerabilities in your platform?
5. Do you provide output filtering for business-specific content categories (e.g., PII
   in outputs, competitor mentions)?
6. How do you handle jailbreak attempts or policy violations in production? Do you
   notify the enterprise customer?

Sub-processor Chain:
7. Which AI model provider(s) do you use? Do you use multiple providers or a single
   primary provider?
8. Do the AI model provider(s) in your sub-processor chain see prompt content? What
   are their data retention commitments?
9. If you fine-tune models on customer data, where is that fine-tuned model stored
   and who has access to it?

Compliance:
10. Do you support GDPR Data Subject Access Requests (DSARs) for data that originated
    from our users? What is your response SLA?
11. Do you offer HIPAA BAA for healthcare deployments? What controls support HIPAA
    compliance in your platform?
12. What certifications cover your AI inference infrastructure specifically (not just
    your corporate IT infrastructure)?
Procurement Process Integration

Vendor assessments are only effective when integrated into the procurement workflow rather than bolted on as a post-decision compliance exercise. By the time commercial negotiations are complete and a vendor is selected, procurement teams are under schedule pressure that makes a thorough security assessment feel like an obstacle. Shifting the assessment left — starting it during the proof of concept or pilot phase — prevents this dynamic.
Pilot phase:

require completion of the basic Tier classification questionnaire before any partner receives access to production data, even for a pilot. A Tier 1 partner that cannot answer basic data handling questions during the pilot should not progress to commercial terms.
Request for Proposal (RFP):

include data handling, security certification, and DPA availability as evaluation criteria in the RFP scoring rubric. This forces vendors to provide security information early and makes it a competitive differentiator.
Legal redline process:
DPA and security exhibit redlines are often the longest-running part of AI vendor contracting. Start the DPA redline process in parallel with commercial term negotiations, not after.
Internal approval gate:

no Tier 1 partner should reach invoice approval without a written sign-off from the InfoSec team and DPO (if applicable). Make this a hard gate in your procurement system rather than a soft recommendation.
Summary

Partner and reseller vendor assessment classifies Claude-adjacent vendors into three risk tiers based on their data exposure: Tier 1 (sub-processors and platform vendors that see prompt and response data), Tier 2 (system integrators and tooling vendors with indirect access), and Tier 3 (resellers and referral partners with no data access). Tier 1 requires a full security assessment including SOC 2, DPA, sub-processor list, and training data opt-out clause. Tier 2 requires a security questionnaire and controls around credential handling and code delivery. Tier 3 requires only commercial verification of reseller status and contract pass-through. All tiers require annual re-assessment, and Tier 1 requires re-assessment after any material security event or scope change.
